variables.content: Numerical derivatives of finite-N DMC energy bands are unstable at k≈k_F: the derivative (dE/dk) evaluated from discrete finite-N DMC data often exhibits outlier points in the immediate vicinity of the Fermi surface, a pathology attributable to the combination of (i) the divergence or pathological behavior of the Hartree-Fock band derivative at k_F and (ii) DMC in a finite cell retrieving only a finite fraction of the exact correlation energy when using a fixed-node Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave function; consequently, robust estimates of the quasiparticle effective mass are obtained by fitting the band over a finite wave-number range Δk about k_F (using, for example, the quartic parametrization E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4) and using the fit-derived derivative at k_F to average over and suppress the near-k_F anomalies.

The quasiparticle effective mass m* is defined from the derivative of the quasiparticle energy band ε(k) at the Fermi wave vector k_F as m* = k_F · (dε/dk)^{-1}_{k_F}, where k_F is the Fermi wave vector and derivatives are evaluated with respect to the magnitude k of the momentum; in atomic units the bare electron mass m equals 1. For the paramagnetic two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) with spin polarization ζ = 0 and density parameter r_s = 1 (where r_s = (1/(πρ))^{1/2} with electron number density ρ), variational Monte Carlo (VMC) and fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit produce values consistent with the bare mass within statistical uncertainty: m*_{VMC}(r_s = 1) = 0.98(1) and m*_{DMC}(r_s = 1) = 0.95(1) (atomic units), indicating negligible mass renormalization from electron–electron interactions at this high density.

For DMC-computed discrete single-quasiparticle bands \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) obtained in finite periodic simulation cells for a 2D HEG, define the pathological behaviour as the erratic variation of estimated quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}\\) when the derivative \\(d\\mathcal{E}/dk\\) is estimated by fitting only a very narrow \\(k\\)-range immediately surrounding the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: this near-\\(k_{F}\\) pathological behaviour of \\(m^{*}\\) estimates arises primarily from finite-cell fluctuations (for example, the discrete sampling of \\(\\mathbf{k}\\)-points and cell-induced finite-size correlation effects) together with Hartree–Fock-like non-smooth mean-field features that persist in finite cells, rather than from an intrinsic non-differentiability or singularity of the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle band at \\(k_{F}\\); this explains why expanding the \\(k\\)-range used in the fit reduces the pathology.

For the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=1, extrapolating finite-N DMC effective masses obtained from quartic-band fits yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.851(5) a.u.; here m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector appropriate to the fully spin-polarized system.

Treating the near-surface accumulated electrons as a two-dimensional (2D) free-electron gas with in-plane effective mass m* = 0.023 m0 (m0 denotes the free-electron mass, m0 ≈ 9.109×10^-31 kg) and with the Fermi energy E_F located 0.2 eV above the bulk conduction-band minimum (CBM), the sheet electron density n_s computed from the 2D free-electron relation n_s = m* E_F/(π ħ^2) is approximately 10^12 cm^-2.

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=10, extrapolating finite-N DMC effective masses obtained from quartic-band fits gives the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=1.03(4) a.u., where m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}.

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=5, the same extrapolation procedure applied to the DMC quartic-fit band data yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=1.04(2) a.u., with m^* defined as m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}.

High-resolution angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements of the hydrogen-dosed ZnO(10ī0) surface show that the surface-localized two-dimensional electron system (2DES) has a momentum-resolved energy-distribution-curve (EDC) at the Fermi-wavevector with a peak-dip-hump structure and a long spectral tail extending to approximately 600 meV binding energy below the Fermi level (binding energy denotes energy below the experimental Fermi level E_F). The measured Fermi contour is circular with a Fermi-wavevector magnitude k_F = 0.77 nm^{-1}, which for a single spin-degenerate circular band corresponds to a surface sheet electron density n_{2D} = 9.4×10^{12} cm^{-2}. Fitting the extracted quasiparticle dispersion near its minimum by a parabolic band yields a first-subband energy minimum (first 2D quasiparticle band bottom) at E_{1} = E_{F} - 86 meV and an effective (phonon-dressed) quasiparticle mass m^{*} = 0.26 m_{e}, where m_{e} is the free-electron mass.

For a two‑dimensional electron gas with sheet density n_{2D} and an isotropic circular Fermi surface occupying a single parabolic band of effective mass m^{*}, the Fermi wavevector is k_F = sqrt(2π n_{2D}), the Fermi velocity is v_F = ħ k_F / m^{*}, and the mobility is μ = e τ / m^{*}; combining L_e = v_F τ with these relations yields L_e = (ħ μ / e) sqrt(2π n_{2D}); this expression assumes a single electronic band with parabolic dispersion in the 2D limit and that n_{2D} counts occupied states in that band.
